question:
Alice and Bob play a game in which they take turns removing stones from
a heap that initially has $n$ stones. The number of stones removed at
each turn must be one less than a prime number. The winner is the player
who takes the last stone. Alice plays first. Prove that there are
infinitely many $n$ such that Bob has a winning strategy.
(For example, if $n=17$, then Alice might take 6 leaving 11; then
Bob might take 1 leaving 10; then Alice can take the remaining stones
to win.)
solution:
Suppose on the contrary that the set $B$ of values of $n$ for which Bob
has a winning strategy is finite; for convenience, we include $n=0$ in $B$,
and write $B = \{b_1, \dots, b_m\}$.
Then for every nonnegative integer $n$ not
in $B$, Alice must have some move on a heap of $n$ stones leading to a
position in which the second player wins. That is, every nonnegative integer
not in $B$ can be written as $b + p - 1$ for some $b \in B$ and some prime
$p$. However, there are numerous ways to show that this cannot happen.

\textbf{First solution:}
Let $t$ be any integer bigger than all of the $b \in B$. Then it is easy to
write down $t$ consecutive composite integers, e.g., $(t+1)! + 2, \dots,
(t+1)! + t+1$. Take $n = (t+1)! + t$; then for each $b \in B$,
$n - b + 1$ is one of the composite integers we just wrote down.

\textbf{Second solution:}
Let $p_1, \dots, p_{2m}$ be
any prime numbers; then by the Chinese remainder theorem, there exists a
positive integer $x$ such that
\begin{align*}
x - b_1 &\equiv -1 \pmod{p_1 p_{m+1}} \\
\dots \\
x - b_n &\equiv -1 \pmod{p_m p_{2m}}.
\end{align*}
For each $b \in B$,
the unique integer $p$ such that $x=b+p-1$ is divisible
by at least two primes, and so cannot itself be prime.

\textbf{Third solution:} (by Catalin Zara)
Put $b_1 = 0$, and take $n = (b_2 - 1)\cdots(b_m - 1)$; then $n$ is
composite because $3, 8 \in B$, and for any nonzero $b \in B$,
$n - b_i + 1$ is divisible by but not equal to $b_i - 1$.
(One could also take $n = b_2 \cdots b_m - 1$, so that
$n-b_i+1$ is divisible by $b_i$.)

descriptive_long.question:
Alice and Bob play a game in which they take turns removing stones from
a heap that initially has $heapcount$ stones. The number of stones removed at
each turn must be one less than a prime number. The winner is the player
who takes the last stone. Alice plays first. Prove that there are
infinitely many $heapcount$ such that Bob has a winning strategy.
(For example, if $heapcount=17$, then Alice might take 6 leaving 11; then
Bob might take 1 leaving 10; then Alice can take the remaining stones
to win.)
descriptive_long.solution:
Suppose on the contrary that the set $bobset$ of values of $heapcount$ for which Bob
has a winning strategy is finite; for convenience, we include $heapcount=0$ in $bobset$,
and write $bobset = \{bobvalone, \dots, bobvalmax\}.
Then for every nonnegative integer $heapcount$ not
in $bobset$, Alice must have some move on a heap of $heapcount$ stones leading to a
position in which the second player wins. That is, every nonnegative integer
not in $bobset$ can be written as $bobvalue + primevar - 1$ for some $bobvalue \in bobset$ and some prime
$primevar$. However, there are numerous ways to show that this cannot happen.

\textbf{First solution:}
Let $lengthblock$ be any integer bigger than all of the $bobvalue \in bobset$. Then it is easy to
write down $lengthblock$ consecutive composite integers, e.g., $(lengthblock+1)! + 2, \dots,
(lengthblock+1)! + lengthblock+1$. Take $heapcount = (lengthblock+1)! + lengthblock$; then for each $bobvalue \in bobset$,
$heapcount - bobvalue + 1$ is one of the composite integers we just wrote down.

\textbf{Second solution:}
Let $primeone, \dots, primedouble$ be
any prime numbers; then by the Chinese remainder theorem, there exists a
positive integer $chinesex$ such that
\begin{align*}
chinesex - bobvalone &\equiv -1 \pmod{primeone \, primeplus} \\
\dots \\
chinesex - bobvaln &\equiv -1 \pmod{p_{setsize} \, primedouble}.
\end{align*}
For each $bobvalue \in bobset$,
the unique integer $primevar$ such that $chinesex=bobvalue+primevar-1$ is divisible
by at least two primes, and so cannot itself be prime.

\textbf{Third solution:} (by Catalin Zara)
Put $bobvalone = 0$, and take $heapcount = (bobvaltwo - 1)\cdots(bobvalmax - 1)$; then $heapcount$ is
composite because $3, 8 \in bobset$, and for any nonzero $bobvalue \in bobset$,
$heapcount - bobvali + 1$ is divisible by but not equal to $bobvali - 1$.
(One could also take $heapcount = bobvaltwo \cdots bobvalmax - 1$, so that
$heapcount-bobvali+1$ is divisible by $bobvali$.)

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
Alice and Bob play a game in which they take turns removing stones from
a heap that initially has $marblebox$ stones. The number of stones removed at
each turn must be one less than a prime number. The winner is the player
who takes the last stone. Alice plays first. Prove that there are
infinitely many $marblebox$ such that Bob has a winning strategy.
(For example, if $marblebox=17$, then Alice might take 6 leaving 11; then
Bob might take 1 leaving 10; then Alice can take the remaining stones
to win.)
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Suppose on the contrary that the set $voyagehub$ of values of $marblebox$ for which Bob
has a winning strategy is finite; for convenience, we include $marblebox=0$ in $voyagehub$,
and write $voyagehub = \{sunflower, \dots, cloudrealm\}$.
Then for every nonnegative integer $marblebox$ not
in $voyagehub$, Alice must have some move on a heap of $marblebox$ stones leading to a
position in which the second player wins. That is, every nonnegative integer
not in $voyagehub$ can be written as $lanterns + quartzine - 1$ for some $lanterns \in voyagehub$ and some prime
$quartzine$. However, there are numerous ways to show that this cannot happen.

\textbf{First solution:}
Let $silktrail$ be any integer bigger than all of the $lanterns \in voyagehub$. Then it is easy to
write down $silktrail$ consecutive composite integers, e.g., $(silktrail+1)! + 2, \dots,
(silktrail+1)! + silktrail+1$. Take $marblebox = (silktrail+1)! + silktrail$; then for each $lanterns \in voyagehub$,
$marblebox - lanterns + 1$ is one of the composite integers we just wrote down.

\textbf{Second solution:}
Let $amberglow, \dots, ivoryflare$ be
any prime numbers; then by the Chinese remainder theorem, there exists a
positive integer $copperveil$ such that
\begin{align*}
copperveil - sunflower &\equiv -1 \pmod{amberglow\, opalcrest} \\
\dots \\
copperveil - riverstone &\equiv -1 \pmod{p_{harborline}\, ivoryflare}.
\end{align*}
For each $lanterns \in voyagehub$,
the unique integer $quartzine$ such that $copperveil=lanterns+quartzine-1$ is divisible
by at least two primes, and so cannot itself be prime.

\textbf{Third solution:} (by Catalin Zara)
Put $sunflower = 0$, and take $marblebox = (moonshard - 1)\cdots(cloudrealm - 1)$; then $marblebox$ is
composite because $3, 8 \in voyagehub$, and for any nonzero $lanterns \in voyagehub$,
$marblebox - stargazer + 1$ is divisible by but not equal to $stargazer - 1$.
(One could also take $marblebox = moonshard \cdots cloudrealm - 1$, so that
$marblebox-lanterns+1$ is divisible by $lanterns$.)

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
Alice and Bob play a game in which they take turns removing stones from
a heap that initially has $boundless$ stones. The number of stones removed at
each turn must be one less than a prime number. The winner is the player
who takes the last stone. Alice plays first. Prove that there are
infinitely many $boundless$ such that Bob has a winning strategy.
(For example, if $boundless=17$, then Alice might take 6 leaving 11; then
Bob might take 1 leaving 10; then Alice can take the remaining stones
to win.)
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
Suppose on the contrary that the set $loserset$ of values of $boundless$ for which Bob
has a winning strategy is finite; for convenience, we include $boundless=0$ in $loserset$,
and write $loserset = \{ultimate, \dots, primordial\}$.
Then for every nonnegative integer $boundless$ not
in $loserset$, Alice must have some move on a heap of $boundless$ stones leading to a
position in which the second player wins. That is, every nonnegative integer
not in $loserset$ can be written as $misfortune + composite - 1$ for some $misfortune \in loserset$ and some prime $composite$. However, there are numerous ways to show that this cannot happen.

\textbf{First solution:}
Let $minuscule$ be any integer bigger than all of the $misfortune \in loserset$. Then it is easy to
write down $minuscule$ consecutive composite integers, e.g., $(minuscule+1)! + 2, \dots,
(minuscule+1)! + minuscule+1$. Take $boundless = (minuscule+1)! + minuscule$; then for each $misfortune \in loserset$,
$boundless - misfortune + 1$ is one of the composite integers we just wrote down.

\textbf{Second solution:}
Let $nonprimal, \dots, nonprimaldouble$ be
any prime numbers; then by the Chinese remainder theorem, there exists a
positive integer $voidness$ such that
\begin{align*}
voidness - ultimate &\equiv -1 \pmod{nonprimal\, nonprimalnext} \\
\dots \\
voidness - primordial &\equiv -1 \pmod{nonprimal\, nonprimaldouble}.
\end{align*}
For each $misfortune \in loserset$,
the unique integer $composite$ such that $voidness=misfortune+composite-1$ is divisible
by at least two primes, and so cannot itself be prime.

\textbf{Third solution:} (by Catalin Zara)
Put $ultimate = 0$, and take $boundless = (penultimate - 1)\cdots(primordial - 1)$; then $boundless$ is
composite because $3, 8 \in loserset$, and for any nonzero $misfortune \in loserset$,
$boundless - specific + 1$ is divisible by but not equal to $specific - 1$.
(One could also take $boundless = penultimate \cdots primordial - 1$, so that
$boundless-specific+1$ is divisible by $specific$.)

garbled_string.question:
Alice and Bob play a game in which they take turns removing stones from
a heap that initially has $zxqplmnae$ stones. The number of stones removed at
each turn must be one less than a prime number. The winner is the player
who takes the last stone. Alice plays first. Prove that there are
infinitely many $zxqplmnae$ such that Bob has a winning strategy.
(For example, if $zxqplmnae=17$, then Alice might take 6 leaving 11; then
Bob might take 1 leaving 10; then Alice can take the remaining stones
to win.)
garbled_string.solution:
Suppose on the contrary that the set $vxhntcosa$ of values of $zxqplmnae$ for which Bob
has a winning strategy is finite; for convenience, we include $zxqplmnae=0$ in $vxhntcosa$,
and write $vxhntcosa = \{jakdvntle, \dots, wvcdjkyua\}.
Then for every nonnegative integer $zxqplmnae$ not
in $vxhntcosa$, Alice must have some move on a heap of $zxqplmnae$ stones leading to a
position in which the second player wins. That is, every nonnegative integer
not in $vxhntcosa$ can be written as $hqrvxsnpt + txqrdvose - 1$ for some $hqrvxsnpt \in vxhntcosa$ and some prime
$txqrdvose$. However, there are numerous ways to show that this cannot happen.

\textbf{First solution:}
Let $ylksdprma$ be any integer bigger than all of the $hqrvxsnpt \in vxhntcosa$. Then it is easy to
write down $ylksdprma$ consecutive composite integers, e.g., $(ylksdprma+1)! + 2, \dots,
(ylksdprma+1)! + ylksdprma+1$. Take $zxqplmnae = (ylksdprma+1)! + ylksdprma$; then for each $hqrvxsnpt \in vxhntcosa$,
$zxqplmnae - hqrvxsnpt + 1$ is one of the composite integers we just wrote down.

\textbf{Second solution:}
Let $klmzgfhpe, \dots, gqtdlzxea$ be
any prime numbers; then by the Chinese remainder theorem, there exists a
positive integer $zbqmrktla$ such that
\begin{align*}
zbqmrktla - jakdvntle &\equiv -1 \pmod{klmzgfhpe \, nvtrsjqae} \\
\dots \\
zbqmrktla - mgztralpe &\equiv -1 \pmod{txqrdvose_{qsldpnria} \, gqtdlzxea}.
\end{align*}
For each $hqrvxsnpt \in vxhntcosa$,
the unique integer $txqrdvose$ such that $zbqmrktla=hqrvxsnpt+txqrdvose-1$ is divisible
by at least two primes, and so cannot itself be prime.

\textbf{Third solution:} (by Catalin Zara)
Put $jakdvntle = 0$, and take $zxqplmnae = (qlmshzrpa - 1)\cdots(wvcdjkyua - 1)$; then $zxqplmnae$ is
composite because $3, 8 \in vxhntcosa$, and for any nonzero $hqrvxsnpt \in vxhntcosa$,
$zxqplmnae - sfkptuqar + 1$ is divisible by but not equal to $sfkptuqar - 1$.
(One could also take $zxqplmnae = qlmshzrpa \cdots wvcdjkyua - 1$, so that
$zxqplmnae-sfkptuqar+1$ is divisible by $sfkptuqar$.)

kernel_variant.question:
Let a single heap initially contain $n\ge 0$ stones.  
On every turn the player whose move it is must choose  

$\;$* a prime number $p$ (the choice $p=2$ is permitted), and  
$\;$* an integer exponent $k\ge 2$

and then remove exactly  
\[
m=p^{\,k}-1
\]
stones from the heap.  
The legal subtraction set is therefore  
\[
\mathcal L=\{\,p^{k}-1:\;p\text{ prime},\;k\ge 2\}
           =\{3,7,8,15,24,26,31,48,\dots\}.
\]
(Observe that $1,2\not\in\mathcal L$.)

Normal play is used (the player making the last move wins) and Alice
moves first.

a)  Prove that there exist infinitely many initial heap sizes $n$ for
which Bob (the second player) has a winning strategy.

b)  Prove the much stronger fact that there are infinitely many
\emph{consecutive} starting sizes that are losing for Alice; that is,
show that there exist infinitely many integers $N$ for which Bob wins
from \emph{both} $N$ and $N+1$.
kernel_variant.solution:
We use standard Sprague-Grundy terminology.  
A position is a $\mathcal P$-position if the previous player can force
a win, and an $\mathcal N$-position otherwise.
Equivalently,

$\;$* a position is $\mathcal N$ iff it has at least one option that is
   $\mathcal P$,  

$\;$* a position is $\mathcal P$ iff \emph{all} of its options are
   $\mathcal N$.  

Let $\mathcal P$ denote the set of $\mathcal P$-positions and write
$a\equiv b\pmod m$ as $a\stackrel{m}{\equiv}b$.
For a finite set $F\subset\mathbb N$ put $\widehat F=\max F$.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
0.  Three trivial $\mathcal P$-positions
--------------------------------------------------------------------
The numbers $0,1,2$ admit no legal move, hence belong to $\mathcal P$.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1.  A basic observation about the move set
--------------------------------------------------------------------
(\star )  If a positive integer $x$ is divisible by two distinct primes, then
$x$ is \emph{not} a prime power.
Consequently, if $y+1$ is divisible by two distinct primes, then
$y\not\in\mathcal L$.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2.  Infinitely many $\mathcal P$-positions (part a)
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Assume for contradiction that
\[
\mathcal P=\{e_1,\dots ,e_t\},\qquad
0=e_1<e_2<\dots<e_t .
\]
Choose pairwise distinct primes
$q_1,\dots ,q_t,r_1,\dots ,r_t$ with every $q_i,r_i>\!e_t$.
By the Chinese Remainder Theorem (CRT) there exists $X$ such that  
\[
X\stackrel{q_i r_i}{\equiv}e_i-1\qquad(1\le i\le t),
\]
and we set  
\[
N:=X+2\!\!\prod_{i=1}^{t}q_i r_i .
\]
For each $i$ we then have
$N-e_i+1\stackrel{q_i r_i}{\equiv}0$, so $N-e_i+1$ is divisible by
$q_i$ and $r_i$ and is therefore not a prime power.
Thus $N-e_i\not\in\mathcal L$ for every $i$, i.e.\ $N$ has no move to
any $e_i$.
All other options are $\mathcal N$ by definition, whence $N$ itself is
$\mathcal P$, contradicting finiteness of $\mathcal P$.
Therefore $\mathcal P$ is infinite, proving (a). \blacksquare 



--------------------------------------------------------------------
3.  A strengthened blocking lemma
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Lemma 1 (strong double blockade).  
Let $F\subset\mathbb N$ be finite.
Then there exists a pair of consecutive integers
\[
(M,M+1),\qquad M>\widehat F,
\]
such that neither $M$ nor $M+1$ has a legal move that ends in $F$.

Proof.  
Assign to every $e\in F$ four fresh primes
$q_e,r_e,s_e,t_e$, all $>\widehat F$ and pairwise distinct.
By the CRT choose $X$ obeying
\[
X\stackrel{q_e r_e}{\equiv}e-1,\qquad
X\stackrel{s_e t_e}{\equiv}e-2\qquad(e\in F),
\]
and set  
\[
M:=X+2\!\!\prod_{e\in F}q_e r_e s_e t_e .
\]
Exactly as in Section 2, for each $e\in F$ both $M-e$ and
$(M+1)-e$ fail to lie in $\mathcal L$, so the claim holds. \blacksquare 



--------------------------------------------------------------------
4.  Consecutive $\mathcal P$-pairs (part b)
--------------------------------------------------------------------
We build an infinite strictly increasing sequence
\[
N_1<N_2<N_3<\dots
\]
such that every pair $(N_j,N_j+1)$ consists of $\mathcal P$-positions.

----------------------------------------------------------------
4.1  Preparation
----------------------------------------------------------------
Let $S_{0}:=\{0,1,2\}$.
For $j\ge 1$ we shall maintain a finite set $S_{j-1}$ which already
\emph{contains every} $\mathcal P$-position not exceeding
$\widehat{S_{j-1}}$.

----------------------------------------------------------------
4.2  Inductive construction
----------------------------------------------------------------
Assume $S_{j-1}$ is available.

Step 1. First try.  
Apply Lemma 1 with $F=S_{j-1}$, obtaining a pair
\[
(M,M+1),\qquad M>\widehat{S_{j-1}},
\]
that cannot move into $S_{j-1}$.

If \emph{both} $M$ and $M+1$ are $\mathcal P$ we set
\[
N_j:=M,\qquad
S_j:=S_{j-1}\cup\{M,M+1\},
\]
and proceed to the next index.

Otherwise at least one of $M,M+1$ is $\mathcal N$.  Let
\[
T:=\{e\le M+1 : e\text{ is }\mathcal P\},\qquad
F':=S_{j-1}\cup T .
\]
The set $F'$ equals \emph{all} $\mathcal P$-positions not exceeding
$M+1$, hence it is finite and satisfies
\[
\widehat{S_{j-1}}\;<\;\widehat{F'}\;\le\;M+1. \tag{1}
\]

Step 2. Further tries.  
Invoke Lemma 1 with $F'$; let the resulting pair be $(M',M'+1)$.
Because $M'>\widehat{F'}$ and by (1) we also have $M'>M+1$.
If both $M',M'+1$ are $\mathcal P$ we record
\[
N_j:=M',\qquad
S_j:=F'\cup\{M',M'+1\}
\]
and stop the inductive step.
If not, enlarge $F'$ by all $\mathcal P$-positions up to $M'+1$ and
repeat Lemma 1.
As $\widehat{F'}$ strictly increases each time, the process is
well defined.

----------------------------------------------------------------
4.3  Termination of the process
----------------------------------------------------------------
Suppose, for a contradiction, that in the $j^{\text{th}}$ induction
stage the repeated application of Lemma 1 never produces a pair of
consecutive $\mathcal P$-positions.
This yields an unbounded sequence
\[
M^{(1)}<M^{(2)}<M^{(3)}<\dots
\]
such that, for every $\nu$,

$\;$* $F_\nu$ (the set fed into the $\nu^{\text{th}}$ call of Lemma 1)
equals \emph{all} $\mathcal P$-positions $\le M^{(\nu)}+1$,  

$\;$* both $M^{(\nu)}$ and $M^{(\nu)}+1$ have no move into $F_\nu$.

Because the sequence $(M^{(\nu)})$ is unbounded, every $\mathcal P$
eventually belongs to some $F_\nu$.
Hence for each $\nu$ the numbers $M^{(\nu)}$ and $M^{(\nu)}+1$ possess
\emph{no} legal move to a lower $\mathcal P$-position.
An $\mathcal N$-position must have at least one such move, so
both $M^{(\nu)}$ and $M^{(\nu)}+1$ are forced to be $\mathcal P$,
contradicting the assumption that no consecutive $\mathcal P$-pair is
ever produced.
Therefore the procedure terminates after finitely many attempts at
every stage $j$.

----------------------------------------------------------------
4.4  Conclusion
----------------------------------------------------------------
Because every inductive stage terminates, we obtain the desired
infinite sequence of consecutive $\mathcal P$-pairs
\[
(N_1,N_1+1),\;(N_2,N_2+1),\;(N_3,N_3+1),\dots,
\]
completing the proof of (b). \blacksquare 



--------------------------------------------------------------------
5.  Final remarks
--------------------------------------------------------------------
1.  The Chinese Remainder Theorem is the only external tool needed.

2.  Inequality (1) corrects the earlier misstated strict inequality;
    only the weaker bound $\widehat{F'}\le M+1$ is required and is now
    explicitly recorded.

3.  Each modulus produced by Lemma 1 recurs periodically, so every pair
    $(N_j,N_j+1)$ generates an infinite arithmetic progression of
    further consecutive $\mathcal P$-pairs.
    Consequently the set of losing starting positions has positive
    lower density.

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
1.  Richer move set.  Legal moves now require the number removed plus 1 to be a prime power pᵏ; analysing positions demands understanding prime powers rather than merely primes or primes shifted by a fixed amount.  
2.  Extra arithmetic structure.  A prime power possesses exactly one distinct prime divisor; exploiting this rigidity forces players to use deeper number-theoretic arguments (CRT combined with multiplicity of prime factors) rather than the simpler “composite vs. prime” dichotomy of the original problem.  
3.  Two goals instead of one.  Part (b) asks not just for infinitely many losing starts for Alice but for an entire arithmetic progression of them, pushing the solver to lift a single-instance argument to a uniform, modular one.  
4.  Heavier theoretical toolkit.  Solving the variant efficiently requires the Chinese Remainder Theorem, the fundamental theorem of arithmetic (to distinguish prime powers from general composites), and game-theoretic reasoning about P- and N-positions; the original could be dispatched with any one of three short elementary tricks.  
These additions elevate both the conceptual and the technical hurdles well beyond those of the original and of the intermediate kernel variant.

original_kernel_variant.question:
Let a single heap initially contain $n\ge 0$ stones.  
On every turn the player whose move it is must choose

3.  A strengthened blocking lemma
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Lemma 1 (strong double blockade).

\[

$(M+1)-e$ fail to lie in $\mathcal L$, so the claim holds. \blacksquare 



--------------------------------------------------------------------
4.  Consecutive $\mathcal P$-pairs (part b)
--------------------------------------------------------------------
We build an infinite strictly increasing sequence
\[
N_1<N_2<N_3<\dots
\]
such that every pair $(N_j,N_j+1)$ consists of $\mathcal P$-positions.

----------------------------------------------------------------
4.1  Preparation
----------------------------------------------------------------
Let $S_{0}:=\{0,1,2\}$.
For $j\ge 1$ we shall maintain a finite set $S_{j-1}$ which already
\emph{contains every} $\mathcal P$-position not exceeding
$\widehat{S_{j-1}}$.

----------------------------------------------------------------
4.2  Inductive construction
----------------------------------------------------------------
Assume $S_{j-1}$ is available.

Step 1. First try.  
Apply Lemma 1 with $F=S_{j-1}$, obtaining a pair
\[
(M,M+1),\qquad M>\widehat{S_{j-1}},
\]
that cannot move into $S_{j-1}$.

If \emph{both} $M$ and $M+1$ are $\mathcal P$ we set
\[
N_j:=M,\qquad
S_j:=S_{j-1}\cup\{M,M+1\},
\]
and proceed to the next index.

Otherwise at least one of $M,M+1$ is $\mathcal N$.  Let
\[
T:=\{e\le M+1 : e\text{ is }\mathcal P\},\qquad
F':=S_{j-1}\cup T .
\]
The set $F'$ equals \emph{all} $\mathcal P$-positions not exceeding
$M+1$, hence it is finite and satisfies
\[
\widehat{S_{j-1}}\;<\;\widehat{F'}\;\le\;M+1. \tag{1}
\]

Step 2. Further tries.  
Invoke Lemma 1 with $F'$; let the resulting pair be $(M',M'+1)$.
Because $M'>\widehat{F'}$ and by (1) we also have $M'>M+1$.
If both $M',M'+1$ are $\mathcal P$ we record
\[
N_j:=M',\qquad
S_j:=F'\cup\{M',M'+1\}
\]
and stop the inductive step.
If not, enlarge $F'$ by all $\mathcal P$-positions up to $M'+1$ and
repeat Lemma 1.
As $\widehat{F'}$ strictly increases each time, the process is
well defined.

----------------------------------------------------------------
4.3  Termination of the process
----------------------------------------------------------------
Suppose, for a contradiction, that in the $j^{\text{th}}$ induction
stage the repeated application of Lemma 1 never produces a pair of
consecutive $\mathcal P$-positions.
This yields an unbounded sequence
\[
M^{(1)}<M^{(2)}<M^{(3)}<\dots
\]
such that, for every $\nu$,

$\;$* $F_\nu$ (the set fed into the $\nu^{\text{th}}$ call of Lemma 1)
equals \emph{all} $\mathcal P$-positions $\le M^{(\nu)}+1$,  

$\;$* both $M^{(\nu)}$ and $M^{(\nu)}+1$ have no move into $F_\nu$.

Because the sequence $(M^{(\nu)})$ is unbounded, every $\mathcal P$
eventually belongs to some $F_\nu$.
Hence for each $\nu$ the numbers $M^{(\nu)}$ and $M^{(\nu)}+1$ possess
\emph{no} legal move to a lower $\mathcal P$-position.
An $\mathcal N$-position must have at least one such move, so
both $M^{(\nu)}$ and $M^{(\nu)}+1$ are forced to be $\mathcal P$,
contradicting the assumption that no consecutive $\mathcal P$-pair is
ever produced.
Therefore the procedure terminates after finitely many attempts at
every stage $j$.

----------------------------------------------------------------
4.4  Conclusion
----------------------------------------------------------------
Because every inductive stage terminates, we obtain the desired
infinite sequence of consecutive $\mathcal P$-pairs
\[
(N_1,N_1+1),\;(N_2,N_2+1),\;(N_3,N_3+1),\dots,
\]
completing the proof of (b). \blacksquare 



--------------------------------------------------------------------
5.  Final remarks
--------------------------------------------------------------------
1.  The Chinese Remainder Theorem is the only external tool needed.

2.  Inequality (1) corrects the earlier misstated strict inequality;
    only the weaker bound $\widehat{F'}\le M+1$ is required and is now
    explicitly recorded.

3.  Each modulus produced by Lemma 1 recurs periodically, so every pair
    $(N_j,N_j+1)$ generates an infinite arithmetic progression of
    further consecutive $\mathcal P$-pairs.
    Consequently the set of losing starting positions has positive
    lower density.

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
1.  Richer move set.  Legal moves now require the number removed plus 1 to be a prime power pᵏ; analysing positions demands understanding prime powers rather than merely primes or primes shifted by a fixed amount.  
2.  Extra arithmetic structure.  A prime power possesses exactly one distinct prime divisor; exploiting this rigidity forces players to use deeper number-theoretic arguments (CRT combined with multiplicity of prime factors) rather than the simpler “composite vs. prime” dichotomy of the original problem.  
3.  Two goals instead of one.  Part (b) asks not just for infinitely many losing starts for Alice but for an entire arithmetic progression of them, pushing the solver to lift a single-instance argument to a uniform, modular one.  
4.  Heavier theoretical toolkit.  Solving the variant efficiently requires the Chinese Remainder Theorem, the fundamental theorem of arithmetic (to distinguish prime powers from general composites), and game-theoretic reasoning about P- and N-positions; the original could be dispatched with any one of three short elementary tricks.  
These additions elevate both the conceptual and the technical hurdles well beyond those of the original and of the intermediate kernel variant.
